coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of dipicolinic acid based on Microporous Ln/melamine-terephthaladehyde schiff base networks complex
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Linli
Song, Yonghai
Xiao, Yingjie
Wu, Rong
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: Lanthanide-based fluorescence sensor in the detection of major Anthrax biomarker dipicolinic acid (DPA) is attracting wide attention. In this work, we proposed a new strategy for ratiometric fluorescence detection of DPA based on microporous Ln/melamine-terephthaladehyde Schiff base networks (Ln/MTSNW) complex for the first time. The microporous MTSNW was prepared by amine-aldehyde condensation between melamine and terephthaladehyde and presented lamellar and octahedral structure. Lanthanide ions, Eu3+ or Tb3+ were coordinated with N atoms of MTSNW to form Ln/MTSNW complex. The microporous Ln/MTSNW complex not only provided large surface area to improve the sensitivity of DPA detection, but also constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensors to eliminate environmental effects and instrument fluctuation. DPA was a highly efficient antenna molecule for Eu3+ and Tb3+ and transferred the energy to Eu3+ or Tb3+ to sensitize their fluorescence. The Ln/MTSNW complex were uniformly and stably dispersed in aqueous solution for DPA detection with a linear range from 15 nM to 7 μM and low detection limit of 5.2 nM for Eu/MTSNW and a linear range from 4 nM to 2.5 μM and low detection limit of 1.4 nM for Tb/MTSNW. Due to the simple preparation of Ln/MTSNW complex and low technical requirement, the ratiometric fluorescence DPA sensor based on Ln/MTSNW complex might show great potential in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide
Melamine-terephthaladehyde schiff base networks
Anthrax biomarker
Dipicolinic acid
Ratiometric fluorescence sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of MTSNW and Ln/MTSNW complex
Possible sensing mechanism of Ln/MTSNW complex toward DPA
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of DPA based on Ln/MTSNW complex

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of MTSNW (A), Eu/MTSNW complex (B) and Tb/MTSNW complex (C). (D) XRD spectra of MTSNW, Eu/MTSNW complex and Tb/MTSNW complex.
(A) FTIR spectra of MTSNW, melamine and terephthaladehyde. (B) FTIR spectra of MTSNW, Eu/MTSNW complex and Tb/MTSNW complex.

UV–vis absorbance spectra (A) and fluorescence emission spectra (B) of MTSNW, Eu/MTSNW complex and Tb/MTSNW complex. The excitation wavelength is 270 nm. The inset is corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram.

UV–vis absorbance spectra before and after adding DPA in Eu/MTSNW complex (A) and Tb/MTSNW complex (B) as well as DPA. Fluorescence spectra of Eu/MTSNW complex (C) and Tb/MTSNW complex (D) before and after adding DPA. The excitation wavelength is 270 nm. The insets are corresponding CIE chromaticity diagrams.

Fluorescence spectra of Eu/MTSNW complex (A) and Tb/MTSNW complex (C) in 25 mM Tris-HCl (pH = 7) buffer solution in the presence of different concentration of DPA. Fitting curves of fluorescence intensity of Eu/MTSNW (B) and Tb/MTSNW (D) versus DPA concentration.

Effect of different metal ions, anions, amino acids and ROS on the fluorescence intensity of Eu/MTSNW (A, B) and Tb/MTSNW (C, D) in 25 mM Tris-HCl (pH = 7) with λex at 270 nm. In Fig. 6A and B, the red bars represent addition of DPA (7 μM), DPA (7 μM)+ metal ions (7 μM), DPA (7 μM) + other interfering substances (35 μM), respectively. In Fig. 6C and D, the red bars represent addition of DPA (2.5 μM), DPA (2.5 μM)+metal ions (5 μM), DPA (2.5 μM)+other interfering substances (15 μM), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustrating preparation of the microporous Ln/MTSNW complex.
Detection limits and linear ranges of various fluorescent probes for DPA detection.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of dipicolinic acid based on Microporous Ln/melamine-terephthaladehyde schiff base networks complex

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang, 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Ji'an Hydrology Bureau, 3 Bamboo Lane Road, Ji'an, 343100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Ji’an Hydrology Bureau, 3 Bamboo Lane Road, Ji’an 343100, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lanthanide-based fluorescence sensor in the detection of major Anthrax biomarker dipicolinic acid (DPA) is attracting wide attention. In this work, we proposed a new strategy for ratiometric fluorescence detection of DPA based on microporous Ln/melamine-terephthaladehyde Schiff base networks (Ln/MTSNW) complex for the first time. The microporous MTSNW was prepared by amine-aldehyde condensation between melamine and terephthaladehyde and presented lamellar and octahedral structure. Lanthanide ions, Eu3+ or Tb3+ were coordinated with N atoms of MTSNW to form Ln/MTSNW complex. The microporous Ln/MTSNW complex not only provided large surface area to improve the sensitivity of DPA detection, but also constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensors to eliminate environmental effects and instrument fluctuation. DPA was a highly efficient antenna molecule for Eu3+ and Tb3+ and transferred the energy to Eu3+ or Tb3+ to sensitize their fluorescence. The Ln/MTSNW complex were uniformly and stably dispersed in aqueous solution for DPA detection with a linear range from 15 nM to 7 μM and low detection limit of 5.2 nM for Eu/MTSNW and a linear range from 4 nM to 2.5 μM and low detection limit of 1.4 nM for Tb/MTSNW. Due to the simple preparation of Ln/MTSNW complex and low technical requirement, the ratiometric fluorescence DPA sensor based on Ln/MTSNW complex might show great potential in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Many spores, such as Bacillus Anthrax spores, are very dangerous to humans and animals [1–3]. After inhaling more than 104 Bacillus Anthrax spores, the mortality rate is as high as 75% even after drug therapy within 24–48 h [4,5]. Bacillus Anthrax spores are used as carrier vectors for biochemical weapons or bioterrorism by illegal elements [6,7]. Therefore, it is of great significance to detect Anthrax spores in disease control and counter-terrorism. Dipicolinic acid (DPA) is an important component and major biomarker of Anthrax spores, which accounts for ~ 10% of the dry weight of Anthrax spores and is not sought in other general spores [8]. So the content of Anthrax spores could be evaluated by the concentration of DPA [9,10]. (see Scheme 1 )

In recent years, many methods for detecting DPA have been developed, such as surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [11], electrochemical sensor [12], mass spectrometry, etc [13]. However, these traditional methods usually require long period, cumbersome operation, expensive reagent and sophisticated sample processing, which limit their practical application. Fluorescence sensing, especially lanthanide-based fluorescence sensors have attracted wide attention recently owing to its high sensitivity, simplicity, low cost and visual detection [14–25]. Due to the fast and efficient association of free lanthanide ions or their complexes with DPA to greatly enhance fluorescence properties of lanthanide, the lanthanide-based fluorescence sensors have merit of high sensitivity and selectivity in detecting DPA [15]. So far, fluorescence sensors based on lanthanide ions Eu3+ or Tb3+ functionalized materials such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), graphene quantum dot, semiconducting polymer dots, carbon dots, carbon nanotubes, polyvinyl alcohol film, polymer nanoparticles, micelle nanoprobe, etc., have been established, showing excellent performance in DPA detection [1,9,17–23]. For example, Zhang et al. reported detection of an anthrax biomarker by regulating energy transfer process in bio-MOFs (Tb/Eu@bio-MOF-1) [17]. Li et al. reported a fluorescent sensor based on semiconducting polymer dots chelated with Tb3+ to detect bacterial spores in aqueous solution [18]. As far as we know, most of the lanthanide-based fluorescence sensors for detecting DPA are according to a single fluorescence intensity change of lanthanide [20–26]. The results are easily interfered by some external factors including environment and instrument. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors show better accuracy and reproducibility as compared to monochromatic fluorescence sensors. The built-in corrected fluorescence reference can eliminate the influence of objective factors such as operation or environmental change [27–31], thus improving the sensitivity and accuracy of sensor [9]. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors for quantitative detection through the linear relationship between the concentration of analyte and the ratio of detection signal and reference signal, has been widely used in microanalysis and trace analysis.

Porous organic polymers are usually consisted of light elements (C, H, O, N), connecting by strong covalent bonds. They have the advantages of high specific surface area, low density, high thermal stability and chemical stability [32]. By selecting suitable monomer and different polymerization reaction, the pore size and structure of porous materials can be manually controlled and a variety of functional groups can be introduced into the pore surface. They are widely used in gas storage, separation, heterogeneous catalysis and luminescence sensing. In 2009, Thomas et al. [33] synthesized a series of new Schiff base networks (SNW) by reaction between melamine and polysubstituted aromatic aldehydes without catalyst. Recently, some researchers have been working on the development and utilization of SNW nanomaterials. Fusheng Pan et al. reported SNW as carriers to immobilize metal ions to facilitate transport membranes for organic solvent separation [34]. Jung Woon Yoon et al. reported highly selective adsorption of CO over CO2 in a Cu(I)-chelated SNW [35]. Wang Zhang et al. reported SNW for trace-level detection of nitroaromatic explosives [36]. However, the research of SNW for DPA detection is not reported so far.

Herein, we used lanthanide ions Eu3+ or Tb3+ to coordinate with N atoms of microporous melamine-terephthaladehyde SNW (MTSNW) which was prepared by amine-aldehyde condensation between melamine and terephthaladehyde to form Ln/MTSNW complex by one-step solvothermal method. A ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Ln/MTSNW complex was developed with the fluorescence of MTSNW as the reference and the fluorescence of lanthanide ions as the receptor for identifying DPA. Eu3+ and Tb3+ have narrow and sharp emission peaks, long fluorescent life and low poisonousness [37–40]. DPA as chromophores sensitized the luminescence of Eu3+ and Tb3+ via absorbing light and transferring energy to Eu3+ and Tb3+ (the so-called antenna effect) [15], but had little impact on the fluorescence of MTSNW. The high specific surface area and good dispersibility of Ln/MTSNW complex provided abundant active sites for DPA, facilitating diffusion of DPA in pores, which made the fluorescence probes more effective and fully contact with DPA. The ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on Ln/MTSNW complex, which can be uniformly and stably dispersed in aqueous solution, exhibited excellent accuracy and sensitivity to DPA detection.

Melamine, terephthalaldehyde, Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, Tb(NO3)3·6H2O, DPA, dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO) and 2-amino-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-propanediol (Tris) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Metal salts and amino acids (Cly, Lys, Glu, Cys) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Other reagents were purchased from Guangdong Xilong Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). All reagents were used as purchased without further purification.

Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer, scanning speed of 1200 nm min−1, and an optical filter of 390 nm. UV–vis absorbance spectra were recorded by UV–vis spectrometer (Hitachi U–3900H). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were collected by a Hitachi S3400 N microscope with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) to give the product morphology and elemental composition. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured on Avatar 360 FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were taken by a Bruker D8 Advanced X-ray powder diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Zeta potential was collected by a Malvern ZEN 3600. Commission International de l’Eclairage (CIE) color coordinates were calculated according to international CIE standards.

The MTSNW was prepared following the reported procedure [41]. The detailed procedure was shown in Supporting Information. The Eu/MTSNW complex was obtained as followed: melamine (156.5 mg), terephthalaldehyde (250 mg) and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (50 mg) were dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (10 mL), and then ultrasounded for 1 h. The solution was poured into a Teflon autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 180 °C, heating for 10 h, and then slowly cooled to room temperature. The jelly-like product was collected by centrifugation at 10000 rpm and washed alternately with DMSO and ethanol for 3 times to remove unreacted reagents. The grayish white powder was obtained by drying in vacuum for 24 h (60 °C). The Tb/MTSNW complex was synthesized in a similar procedure to Eu/MTSNW, except Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (50 mg) was replaced by Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (50 mg).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MTSNW and Ln/MTSNW complex
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, SEM images of MTSNW, Eu/MTSNW complex and Tb/MTSNW complex were shown in Fig. 1 A–C to evaluate their morphology. SEM image of MTSNW showed octahedral structure with the diameters of around 1.5 μm growing on lamellar layer (Fig. 1A). SEM images of Eu/MTSNW complex (Fig. 1B) and Tb/MTSNW complex (Fig. 1C) revealed uniform particle with size of about 300 nm. Due to the coordination between Eu3+, Tb3+ and MTSNW, MTSNW was changed to small particles. XRD pattern of MTSNW showed a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 22°, indicating that the MTSNW was amorphous with low crystallinity (Fig. 1D) [42]. The XRD spectra of Eu/MTSNW and Tb/MTSNW were consistent with that of MTSNW, and no new peak appeared (Fig. 1D), indicating that Eu/MTSNW and Tb/MTSNW were still amorphous, and the coordination of Ln3+ did not change the crystallinity of MTSNW.

The bond formation and chemical composition of MTSNW, Eu/MTSNW and Tb/MTSNW were determined by FTIR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 A, FTIR spectrum of melamine exhibited H–N–H stretching vibration bands at 3417 cm−1 and 3468 cm−1 and H–N–H deformation bands at 1650 cm−1 [33]. FTIR spectrum of terephthalaldehyde exhibited the CO and C–H stretching vibration bands at 1691 cm−1 and 2864 cm−1, respectively. All the above bands attenuated or disappeared in FTIR spectrum of MTSNW, indicating the condensation of amino groups with aldehyde groups [41]. After Eu3+ or Tb3+ coordinated with MTSNW to form Ln/MTSNW, the stretching vibration peak of C–N was shifted from 1338 cm−1–1346 cm−1, out-of-plane bending vibration peak of N–H at 876 cm−1 weakened, verifying the coordination (Fig. 2B). Fig. S1 (Supporting Information) showed XPS spectra of Eu/MTSNW and Tb/MTSNW. The peaks at around 1135 eV and 1243 eV were assigned to the 3d transition of Eu (Fig. S1A) and Tb (Fig. S1B), respectively. For Eu/MTSNW complex, the atomic concentrations were 43.48% C, 34.07% N, 21.48% O and 0.98% Eu (Inset in Fig. S1A). For Tb/MTSNW complex, the atomic concentrations were 45.85% C, 34.88% N, 18.35% O and 0.92% Tb (Inset in Fig. S1B). The result confirmed that Eu3+ and Tb3+ were successfully coordinated with MTSNW. The peaks of Eu3+ and Tb3+ in EDS also indicated that the formation of Ln/MTSNW complex (Fig. S2, Supporting Information).

N2 adsorption/desorption experiments revealed pore size distribution and Brunauer–Emmet– Teller (BET) surface area (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). The MTSNW and Ln/MTSNW showed hierarchical pore from micropores to mesopores, which were estimated by Barrett–Joyner– Halenda (BJH) methods. The average pore width of MTSNW, Eu/MTSNW and Tb/MTSNW were 12.16, 4.62, 5.04 nm, respectively. The coordination of Eu3+ or Tb3+ resulted in the reduction of pore width, indicating the coordination of Eu3+ and Tb3+ with nitrogen atoms. The BET surface area of MTSNW, Eu/MTSNW and Tb/MTSNW were calculated to be 240, 320 and 318 m2/g, respectively. The increase of BET surface area of Ln/MTSNW as compared with MTSNW might be due to the change of morphology. TGA (Fig. S4, Supporting Information) showed that the mass decreased sharply from 400 °C, indicating that thermal stability of MTSNW, Eu/MTSNW and Tb/MTSNW were about at 400 °C. The zeta potential of MTSNW was measured as −1.10 mV, while that of Eu/MTSNW and Tb/MTSNW were as high as −9.48 and −8.21 mV, respectively. The change of zeta potential indicated that the coordination of Eu3+, Tb3+ enhanced the solubility of Ln/MTSNW complex in aqueous solution. In addition, the Ln/MTSNW fluorescence remained basically unchanged for one week, showing high day-to-day stability in aqueous solution (Fig. S5, Supporting Information).

As shown in Fig. 3 A, UV–vis absorption spectra to MTSNW, Eu/MTSNW and Tb/MTSNW showed a peak at about 250 nm, 265 nm, 265 nm, respectively, which were mainly ascribed to the π-π* transition of MTSNW. Notably, UV–vis absorption peak of Ln/MTSNW had a significant red-shift as compared with MTSNW, which was ascribed to the coordination of Eu3+, Tb3+ with nitrogen atoms of MTSNW. The luminescence properties of MTSNW and Ln/MTSNW were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B, the maximum emission peak of MTSNW, Eu/MTSNW and Tb/MTSNW were observed at 450 nm with λex at 270 nm. Since fluorescence of Eu3+ and Tb3+ were quenched by coordinated H2O [23], Ln/MTSNW emitted only the blue fluorescence of MTSNW (Inset in Fig. 3B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible sensing mechanism of Ln/MTSNW complex toward DPA
ce:section.ce:para: To explore sensing mechanism of Ln/MTSNW complex toward DPA, their UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were measured before and after the adding of DPA. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, the ultraviolet absorption of Ln/MTSNW + DPA red-shifted as compared with MTSNW and Ln/MTSNW (from 250 nm/265 nm–270 nm), close to that of DPA. Owning to the quenching induced by high-frequency O–H oscillators from coordinated H2O, Ln/MTSNW only exhibited the fluorescence of MTSNW (Fig. 4C and D) [23]. However, after the addition of DPA, the fluorescence of Eu3+ and Tb3+ were emitted and the fluorescence intensity of MTSNW remained unchanged (Fig. 4C and D). Therefore, Ln/MTSNW complex could be used as potential ratiometric luminescence nanoprobe for the detection of DPA. The f-f transition of lanthanide ions was partly forbidden, so lanthanide ions had weak ability and low efficiency in luminescence upon direct excitation. However, a chromophore called “antenna” could sensitize the luminescence of lanthanide ions by chelating and transferring energy to them [15]. The carboxyl group and nitrogen atoms of DPA could strongly coordinate with Eu3+ and Tb3+, which replaced coordinated H2O in Ln/MTSNW. It greatly minimized the nonradiative quenching of Eu3+ and Tb3+ by removing the high frequency O–H oscillators from coordinated H2O. Furthermore, the lowest triplet energy level of DPA promoted the transfer of energy to Eu3+ or Tb3+. DPA with π-π conjugated structure was a highly efficient antenna molecule for Eu3+ and Tb3+, which effectively sensitized the luminescence of Eu3+ and Tb3+ by absorbing the excitation light (270 nm) and transferring energy. The sensing mechanism of Ln/MTSNW complex toward DPA just were based on the absorbance-energy transfer-emission effect from DPA to Eu3+ and Tb3+ [23]. The Eu/MTSNW complex displayed three emission peaks at 450, 593 and 616 nm in the presence of DPA (Fig. 4C), which was caused by the emission of MTSNW (450 nm) and [Eu3+-DPA] complex (593 and 614 nm) (Inset). The two peaks at 593 and 614 nm were assigned to the transition of Eu3+ excited states 5D0-7FJ (J = 1, 2) (Fig. S6A, Supporting Information) [43]. The strongest and sharpest red fluorescence of Eu3+ (5D0-7F2 at 614 nm) was served as response signal. The Tb/MTSNW complex (Fig. 4D) showed five emission peaks at 450, 490, 543, 584, and 620 nm in DPA solution, which was caused by the emission of MTSNW (450 nm) and [Tb3+-DPA] complex (490, 543, 584 and 620 nm) (Inset). The four peaks at 490, 543, 584 and 620 nm were assigned to the transition of Tb3+ excited states 5D4-7FJ (J = 6, 5, 4, 3) [44–47] (Fig. S6B, Supporting Information). The strongest and sharpest green fluorescence of Tb3+ (5D4-7F5 at 543 nm) was served as the detection signal of DPA. The emission peak of MTSNW (450 nm) was basically unchanged, so the fluorescence of MTSNW was a good candidate for reference. The Ln/MTSNW complex was used for ratiometric fluorescence detection of DPA by a self-calibrated reliable way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of DPA based on Ln/MTSNW complex
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain high sensitivity in detecting DPA, the excitation wavelength, pH and reaction time were explored. The excitation spectra showed that the maximum excitation wavelength were both 270 nm with maximum emission peaks of Eu3+ at 614 nm and Tb3+ at 543 nm. The MTSNW emitted moderate fluorescence with λex at 270 nm (Fig. S7, Supporting Information). Thus, the excitation wavelength of 270 nm was selected for ratiometric fluorescence analysis. As shown in Fig. S8 (Supporting Information), the influence of pH on the detection of DPA was studied. The fluorescence of MTSNW was not affected by pH, while the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ and Tb3+ sensitized by DPA were affected by pH. The fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ and Tb3+ increased firstly and then reduced with the increase of pH, and reached the maximum at pH 7. Under acidic conditions, DPA was protonated, while under alkaline conditions, Eu3+ and Tb3+ tended to precipitate. Therefore, acidic or alkaline environment was not conducive to DPA sensitized Eu3+ and Tb3+ fluorescence. So pH 7 was chosen as the conditions for determination of DPA. The time-dependent fluorescence response of DPA was further monitored (Fig. S9, Supporting Information). The fluorescence intensity was over 80% in 1 min, maintained maximum response after 10 min, and was stable in the next 20 min. So, 10 min was chosen as an optimum reaction time for the subsequent measurements.

The as-prepared Eu/MTSNW or Tb/MTSNW (1.0 mg) was dispersed into 10 mL H2O by ultrasonic treatment. Then 100 μL above suspension was added into 900 μL Tris-HCl buffer solution (25 mM, pH = 7.0). After incubation for 30 min at room temperature, the Eu/MTSNW or Tb/MTSNW solution (10 mg L−1) were prepared. For fluorescence test, freshly prepared DPA standard solution (0–10 μM) was added into the Eu/MTSNW or Tb/MTSNW solution. The mixtures were mixed thoroughly and their fluorescence spectra were measured under 270 nm excitation in the range of 400–700 nm after 10 min of incubation at room temperature. It was observed that Eu3+, Tb3+ emission peak were enhanced gradually with the increase of DPA concentration, while MTSNW emission peak was almost unchanged (Fig. 5 A and C). The F 614/F 450 of Eu/MTSNW had an excellent linear relationship with DPA concentration from 15 nM to 7 μM and low detection limit of 5.2 nM based on a 3σ/slope (Fig. 5B). The F 544/F 450 of Tb/MTSNW had an excellent linear relationship with DPA concentration from 4 nM to 2.5 μM, and low detection limit of 1.4 nM based on a 3σ/slop (Fig. 5D). The detection limits were lower than the maximum concentration of DPA for human being (0.1 μM) [48]. The Ln/MTSNW ratiometric fluorescence sensors were better than that of lanthanide-based MOFs fluorescence sensors [26]. As compared with other lanthanide-based fluorescence sensors (Table 1 ) [10,23,25,49–51], the Ln/MTSNW ratiometric fluorescence sensors showed wider linear range, lower detection limit and higher sensitivity. More importantly, hydrophilic Ln/MTSNW complex could directly detect DPA in aqueous solution, while some sensors listed in Table 1 must be in organic solvent. The large specific surface areas of microporous Ln/MTSNW complex increased the chance of contact with DPA, improving the sensitivity. The fluorescence emission peak of MTSNW as internal parameters could overcome the errors caused by environmental effects [52]. As an excellent template for fluorescence probe, the MTSNW played a key role in the sensitive and precise detection of DPA by ratiometric method. At the same time, due to their unique hierarchically porous structures, the fluorescence of porous Ln/MTSNW complex exhibited high day-to-day stability. The excellent stable fluorescence make Ln/MTSNW complex be a good fluorescence probe.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of Ln/MTSNW complex in detecting DPA, sensors in the presence of different interfering substances under the above selected experimental conditions were investigated, such as metal ions Mn+ (M = Hg2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ag+, Sn2+, Fe3+), anions Xn− (X = F−, I−, Br−, S2−) (Fig. 6 A and C), amino acids (Cly, Lys, Glu, Cys) and reactive oxygen species (ROS, such as H2O2, ClO−, O2 .-, 1O2, ONOO−) (Fig. 6B and D). These ROS were prepared following the reported procedure [53]. The detailed procedures were shown in Supporting Information. Except for ClO−, the coexistence of these substances had little effect on F 614/F450 and F 543/F 450, and only DPA significantly enhanced F 614/F 450 and F 543/F 450. When the concentration of ClO− was six times that of DPA, ClO− + DPA resulted in slight decrease in fluorescence intensity of F 543/F 450. In fact, 10 μM ClO− (four times that of DPA) had little influence on F 543/F 450. Competition experiments showed that the luminescent response of Eu/MTSNW to DPA was not disturbed by metal ions (7 μM) and other interfering substances (35 μM), Tb/MTSNW to DPA was not disturbed by metal ions (5 μM), ClO− (10 μM) and other interfering substances (15 μM). The sensing systems have good selectivity for the detection of DPA because the carboxyl group and nitrogen atoms in pyridine ring of DPA strongly coordinated with Eu3+ and Tb3+ . DPA replaced the coordinated H2O from Eu3+ and Tb3+ center, transferring energy to lanthanide ions. The lowest triplet level of DPA (3π-π*) and Eu3+, Tb3+ energy levels matched well [54]. So, DPA sensitized the intrinsic fluorescence of lanthanide ions.

We further evaluated the applicability of Ln/MTSNW complex for DPA detection in fetal calf serum samples. The result was validated with high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). Different concentrations of DPA were added to diluted serum samples (1/100) by standard addition method. The experiment results were shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information) with good recoveries, indicating that ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on Ln/MTSNW complex were accurate and credible.

In summary, ratiometric fluorescence detection of major Anthrax biomarker DPA based on the microporous Ln/MTSNW complex was proposed for the first time. The microporous MTSNW was an excellent template for fluorescence probe, emitting characteristic blue fluorescence for ratiometric sensing. The Ln/MTSNW complex not only provided large surface area to improve the sensitivity of DPA detection, but also constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensors to eliminate the environmental effects and instrument fluctuation. Hydrophilic Ln/MTSNW complex as fluorescence probe have the advantages of simple preparation, low technical requirement, high accuracy and sensitivity, and good repeatability for the detection of DPA. The microporous Ln/MTSNW complex were uniformly and stably dispersed in aqueous solution for the detection of DPA with a linear range from 15 nM to 7 μM and low detection limit of 5.2 nM for Eu/MTSNW and with a linear range from 4 nM to 2.5 μM and low detection limit of 1.4 nM for Tb/MTSNW. It might have potential application for the detection of DPA in cells and provide relevant basis for the prevention of related biological diseases and bioterrorism.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21465015 and 21665012), Youth Science and Technology Innovation Fund of Jiangxi Hydrographic Bureau (SWJJ-KT 201805).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120534.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A nanoscaled lanthanide metal–organic framework as a colorimetric fluorescence sensor for dipicolinic acid based on modulating energy transfer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, B. Li, H. Ma, L. Zhang, H. Jiang, H. Song, L. Zhang, Y. Luo, A nanoscaled lanthanide metal-organic framework as a colorimetric fluorescence sensor for dipicolinic acid based on modulating energy transfer, J. Mater. Chem. C 4 (2016) 7294-7301.
